course_description:
Design, implementation and presentation of an extensive research project, approved by the Head of School and relevant to the course, with emphasis on defining the research issue, establishing methodology, undertaking appropriate analysis of data and drawing defensible conclusions. The project is completed full-time across 2 semesters.

Students must submit the Research Project Contract by the first Thursday of Teaching Week 1 in February for students commencing in Semester 1, and by the first Thursday of Teaching Week 1 in July for students commencing in Semester 2.

Contact agriculture@enquire.uq.edu.au for enrolment queries.

This course is part of the requirements of the program and is designed to introduce the students to research methods. Students will develop skills to undertake the research and report their findings in the form of a research thesis.

Please note these steps to secure a Research Project .

1. The student contacts potential academic supervisors working in areas that they are interested in to arrange a topic for their research project.

2. The student then completes a learning contract with the project supervisor, to be submitted to StudentHub by the first Thursday of Teaching Week 1.

At the School of Agriculture and Food Sustainability, we are committed to creating an inclusive and empowering learning environment for all students. We value and respect the diverse range of experiences our students bring to their education, and we believe that this diversity is crucial for fostering a rich culture of knowledge-sharing and meaningful exploration. We hold students and staff accountable for actively contributing to establishing a respectful and supportive learning environment.

Bullying, harassment, and discrimination in any form are strictly against our principles and againstﾠ UQ Policy, ﾠand will not be tolerated. If you have any concerns about your experience in this course, we encourage you to tell a member of the course teaching team. Your well-being and a positive learning atmosphere are of utmost importance to us.

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
Completion of 8-units of study in the programs.
restrictions: Masters of Food Science and Technology Research Extensive Students (Program Code 5753)

course_contacts.name: Dr Marlize Bekker
course_contacts.notes: Consultation is by appointment. To make an appointment, please email.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
There will be an introductory session in Week 1 of Semester 1, and an advisory session in Week 8 of Semester 2.
There will also be a two-day in-person symposium for the oral presentations in Week 13 of Semester 2.
If you have any concerns regarding your official timetable or course allocations, or if there are no suitable class times available, contact the Faculty of Science Timetable at
science.mytimetable@uq.edu.au
for advice.
If you have any questions about missing class activities, please contact your course coordinator.
course_aims: To introduce students to the activities involved in carrying out a research project.

learning_outcomes.description: Define a research problem, state a research hypothesis, and plan a process for the investigation of the problem.

Integrate the practical experience gained in previous years of study to develop a novel process, product, analyse the various aspects of food and nutrition topics; OR undertake fundamental studies, and research data recording.
Design and implement a research project/survey; OR undertaking a substantial critical literature review.
Analyse, process, and interpret research data.
Present experimental results and draw relevant statistical conclusions.
Write a thesis in an approved format.
Deliver an oral presentation of a research project.

assessment_summary.title: Research Defence Presentation
assessment_summary.due_date: Week 26 Mon - Week 26 Fri; Day and time to be advised.

assessment_summary.title: Research Project Final Report and Performance

assessment_details.title: Research Proposal
assessment_details.task_description: Students are required to submit a research proposal and experimental protocol to their primary project adviser to allow assessment and feedback of progress with respect to their level of scientific technical planning, writing skills, knowledge depth and coverage, and comprehension. The research proposal guidelines are below and can also be found attached and under Course Files on the Blackboard site. Structure of a Research Proposal (~2000 words) 1. Introduction to the topic 2. Hypothesis 3. Aims and Objectives 4. Methodology 5. Timeline (GANTT Chart) 6. References (APA 7th) Introduction (30%) Aims, research questions and hypotheses. Short literature review. The relevant theory is described briefly. Clear and concise statements explaining the rationale for the research are provided. The rationale is both understandable and convincing. The proposal contains clear and concise statements of research aims, objectives and hypotheses. The research question logically follows from the extant literature and addresses a meaningful gap or debate in the field. Methods (40%) The methods and materials are appropriate to answer the research question and the study design, and are reliable and valid. The description of the procedure is thorough, logical, and coherent, providing sufficient detail to enable replication. All stages involved in conducting the research are clearly described, and the approach is feasible. The analysis strategy (can be statistics or other relevant analysis) is clearly described and optimal in addressing the research question(s). Limitations of the approach are considered and addressed. Gantt chart (20%) Thoughtful and realistic timeline; incorporates all necessary tasks for project completion. Presentation (10%) Spelling and grammar are appropriate, visual aids (figures, graphs, tables, etc.) are suitable for the project under consideration, the format has been followed correctly, and the correct reference format using APA 7th. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of the assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission, independent of AI and MT tools. The use of AI or MT must be referenced appropriately. Please see the assessment guidelines in the Blackboard course site for specific instructions. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Turnitin submission details:
You must submit your Assessment task, in Blackboard, via the Turnitin link by the submission deadline. You should also retain an electronic copy of every piece of assessment you submit.
Legal Declaration:
By submitting your work via this website, you formally declare that (1) it is your own original work, and no part of the work has been copied from any other source or person except where due acknowledgement is made; (2) no part of the work has been previously submitted for assessment in this or any other institution; and (3) you have read the Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and understand its implications.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Submission of an Extension of Assessment Due Date (EADD) should be completed online through your My Requests tab via my.UQ Dashboard , in accordance with the University's Assessment Procedure (located in the Policies and Procedures section of this course profile) which outlines the requirements for requesting extensions to assessment due dates. Extensions require the correct evidence/documentation to support the requested length of time of the extension.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
For example, a report worth a maximum of 40 marks, submitted 28 hours late will attract a penalty of 8 marks, calculated as 2 periods of 24 hours x 10% x 40 marks.
Assessment deadlines are firm and must be met. Late submission without permission or non-submission of assessable work will result in penalties being applied . Late submission of assessment will only be granted for documented medical reasons, accident, bereavement, jury service and other circumstances allowed in the UQ Policies and Procedures Library (PPL).

assessment_details.title: Research Defence Presentation
assessment_details.due_date: Week 26 Mon - Week 26 Fri Day and time to be advised.
assessment_details.task_description: A presentation will be an in-person presentation consisting of 10-minute presentation and 5 minutes of questions. The following presentation format is recommended. 1. Title page - 1 slide 2. Outline - 1 slide 3. Background - aims, hypothesis and objectives - 1 slide 4. Materials and methods - 2 slides 5. Results - 4 slides 6. Conclusion and recommendation for further research - 1 slide. The presentation will be recorded. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of the assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission, independent of AI and MT tools. Please refer to the assessment guidelines on the Blackboard course site for specific instructions. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Research Project Final Report and Performance
assessment_details.task_description: See final report format recommendations (manuscript structure) below and on the Blackboard site. An example of a manuscript is also available on the Blackboard site. The final report should be written and formatted as a peer-reviewed manuscript for submission to a scientific journal consisting of approximately 7000 to 8000 words. The report should demonstrate the student’s ability to conduct scientific research and communicate their findings clearly and professionally. Start by selecting a reputable journal in your research field. Review the journal's author guidelines carefully and adhere to its specific formatting, citation style, and submission requirements. Clearly identify which journal style is followed for the preparation of the manuscript. Use the structure outlined below as guidance for preparing the manuscript and adhere to the formatting guidelines outlined by the selected journal to ensure the manuscript aligns with the requirements of academic publishing. 1. Manuscript Structure Your manuscript must include the following sections: Title Page Title of the journal Title: A concise and informative title that accurately reflects your study. Author(s): Include your name(s) and institutional affiliation(s). Corresponding Author: Provide an email address for correspondence. Abstract (250–300 words) Provide a brief summary of the research, including the background, objectives, methodology, key findings, and conclusions. Use clear and concise language without references or citations. Keywords (3–6 keywords) Select relevant keywords that reflect the key themes of your research. Introduction Introduce the research problem and its significance. Provide a review of the relevant literature to contextualise your study. State the research objectives and hypothesis (if applicable). Materials and Methods Describe the experimental design, materials used, and procedures followed in sufficient detail for reproducibility. Include any ethical considerations or permissions obtained. Specify analytical techniques, software, and statistical methods used. Results Present your findings in a logical sequence. Utilise tables, figures, and graphs to effectively communicate your data. Avoid interpreting results in this section. Discussion Interpret your results in the context of existing literature. Discuss the implications of your findings, limitations of your study, and potential future research directions. Ensure your discussion aligns with your research objectives. Conclusion Summarise your main findings and their significance. Avoid introducing new data or concepts. References Use a consistent citation style as directed by the journal guidelines (e.g., APA 7th or the journal’s preferred format). Include all references cited in the text. Supplementary Materials (if applicable) Include any additional data, figures, or appendices that support your study. 2. Additional Guidelines Use a formal, scientific tone throughout your manuscript. Write in the third person and past tense for the methods and results. Formatting: Use Times New Roman, 12 pt as font Use double spacing. Number the pages and include section headings. For those students who have difficulty in deciding what page format to use, the following is a suggestion: Margins: Top 3 cm, Bottom 3 cm, Left 3 cm, Right 3 cm. Paragraphs: Left justified, Right unjustified, Indent first line by 1.5 cm. Figures and tables: Figures and tables should be appropriately labelled and referenced in the text. Include clear, descriptive captions for all figures and tables, positioned either above (tables) or below (figures) the item. Proofreading: Carefully proofread your report for grammar, spelling, and formatting errors before submission. Submit your proof to your supervisor to receive feedback on how to improve your thesis before submission. Marking of the Thesis The supervisor and an independent marker who specialise in your area of research will grade your work. The average of your marks from both markers will be used as your grade. In the event that there is a difference of more than 10 points between markers, your report will be sent to an independent marker and a decision made, which may involve taking the average of the 3 marks. Student Performance Marking The advisor's mark is an objective assessment by the student's advisor(s) of the overall quality of the student in terms of their scholarship, practical competence, and potential for independent postgraduate research. This assessment will be guided by adherence to the scientific practice of maintaining a laboratory or field record through regular entries in a Laboratory Notebook. Laboratory Notebook Project activities must be recorded each week in the Lab Notebook, with at least 1 page of the book allocated for each week of activities. A Laboratory Notebook is available to students at the start of their course. Each student is required to submit their Laboratory Notebook and all project data to their supervisor on the thesis submission due date. The student and supervisor must sign a declaration as formal confirmation that the project data and information transfer have been completed. This document must be submitted to the School of Agriculture and Food Sustainability Student Services by the due date, along with the Thesis. This is a compulsory requirement to pass the course. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of the assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission, independent of AI and MT tools. The use of AI or MT must be referenced appropriately. Please see the assessment guidelines in the Blackboard course site for specific instructions. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 3
learning_activities.topic: Laboratory safety induction All students need to undertake the Health and Safety Inductions and carry out online training before commencing their projects. Laboratory safety training sessions for research project students are scheduled fortnightly and hosted via Zoom. All students will need to: Receive a site induction with their Supervisor or delegate. This must include emergency evacuation exit, assembly area, first aid stations, first aid officer and nearest Defibrillator. Complete a paper based New Worker induction checklist with their supervisor and email to sciohs@uq.edu.au . Complete Training Needs Analysis for all workers (TNA) with their supervisor. Email these to sciohs@uq.edu.au once complete. Complete mandatory online training (identified in the TNA) as soon as possible (i.e. Fire Safety training) through either workday or blackboard. Complete Training Needs Analysis for Lab workers (TNA-lab) with their supervisor. Email these to sciohs@uq.edu.au once complete. Attend a Faculty of Science HSW Safety Training Session To enrol in a Faculty of Science HSW Safety Training Session. To register or email sciohs@uq.edu.au . Learning outcomes: L01

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 26 (23 Feb - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic: Experimental work Students will conduct their research projects under the guidance of their supervisor. All experimental activities must be documented in the laboratory notebook provided by the School of Agriculture and Food Sustainability. Upon completing the research project, the student must transfer all project-related data, records, documentation, and relevant materials to their primary academic supervisor. This includes both raw and processed data, laboratory notebooks, electronic files, reports, and any other project outputs or records generated during the course of the research. The student and supervisor have to sign a declaration as a formal confirmation that the project data and information transfer have been completed, and this document has to be submitted to the School of Agriculture and Food Sustainability Student Services by the thesis due date. This is a compulsory requirement to pass the course. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 26 To Week 26 (26 Oct - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic: Oral Presentation Learning outcomes: L05, L07

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . You'll also need to be aware of the following policies and procedures while completing this course: Occupational Health and Safety in the Laboratory Guideline Vaccinations and Immunisation Procedure Learning activities
